title: Where Naive RAG Fails
concept: Irrelevant chunks, missed multi-hop facts, and no verification that retrieved context actually answers the question.

exercises.prompt: What are the core steps of the basic 'Naive RAG' pipeline we covered in the last lesson?

options.text: Chunking, embedding, retrieving, and stuffing into the LLM prompt
Fine-tuning the LLM directly on user documents without using database search
Translating user queries into SQL commands to fetch structured rows

exercises.explain: The classic Naive RAG pipeline starts by dividing documents into chunks, generating embedding vectors, storing them, and then retrieving the closest matches to stuff into the prompt context.

cards.title: The Garbage In, Garbage Out Trap
cards.body: While Naive RAG is easy to build, it is easily confused. When your documents get complex, a simple search-and-stuff approach starts feeding your AI garbage data or missing the point entirely.

cards.title: The Limits of Simple RAG
cards.body: Naive RAG fails when it retrieves irrelevant chunks that distract the LLM, misses connection points in multi-hop questions, and fails to verify if the retrieved text actually contains the answer.

cards.title: The Clueless Librarian
cards.body: Imagine asking a librarian a complex riddle. A basic librarian just grabs any books containing the words from your question, dumps them on your desk, and walks away without checking if the books actually answer your riddle.

cards.title: A Multi-Hop Failure
cards.body: If you ask, 'What color is the car owned by the winner of the 2023 race?', Naive RAG might find chunks about the race winner, but fail to retrieve the separate chunk mentioning what car that person drives.

cards.title: The Similarity Trap
cards.body: Many believe that if your vector database returns top matches, they must be useful. In reality, text can be highly similar in vocabulary but completely useless for answering your specific question.

cards.title: Blind Trust vs. Verification
cards.body: Comparing how Naive RAG handles a query versus how a smart system verifies its sources.

left.text: Finds similar-sounding text, stuffs it into the prompt, and hopes for the best.

right.text: Checks if the retrieved text actually contains the answer before generating.

exercises.prompt: Why does Naive RAG often pass useless information to the LLM?

options.text: It automatically rewrites the query to find better documents.
It lacks a verification step to check if the retrieved chunks actually answer the question.
It filters out all irrelevant text before sending it to the LLM.
It alerts the user if the retrieved documents are poor quality.

exercises.explain: Naive RAG blindly feeds whatever it retrieves into the prompt without checking its actual utility, which can lead to halluncinations or irrelevant answers.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a document chunk has a high semantic similarity score to a user's question, it is guaranteed to contain the answer.
exercises.explain: Semantic similarity measures how close words are in vector space, not whether they contain the factual answer to a specific question.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of a major RAG limitation:
exercises.sentence: When an answer requires linking clues across different documents, Naive RAG fails because it cannot handle multi-hop questions.
exercises.explain: A multi-hop query requires connecting multiple pieces of information spread across different documents to find the final answer.

exercises.prompt: Identify the response generated by an LLM confused by irrelevant retrieved chunks:

options.text: The capital of France is Paris. According to the documents, the population is 2.1 million.
I cannot find any information about the 2024 product release date in the provided documents.
Based on the documents about baking recipes, the company's financial revenue in 2023 was 5 cups of sugar.

exercises.explain: Option C shows a classic RAG hallucination/confusion caused by distracting, irrelevant context being stuffed into the prompt, whereas A and B demonstrate successful reasoning.

exercises.prompt: Since Naive RAG fails at 'multi-hop' questions (where you need Fact A to find Fact B), how do advanced RAG architectures solve this problem?

options.text: By increasing the embedding dimension to capture all possible facts at once
By discarding vector databases entirely and using SQL databases instead
By splitting the question into smaller sub-queries and retrieving information in steps
By compressing the entire database into a single system prompt

exercises.explain: Naive RAG struggles with multi-step reasoning (multi-hop queries) because standard vector searches only look for direct word matches. To solve this, Advanced RAG uses techniques like query rewriting, sub-query generation, and agentic loops to break the question down and search step-by-step.
